does: This paper introduces SRTJ, a training-free framework for jailbreaking language models that automatically discovers and refines attack strategies through iterative interaction with target models. The core contribution is a systematic approach that combines experience-driven attack generation with answer set programming-based rule selection to compose reusable jailbreak rules without modifying model parameters. The method learns from both successful and failed attacks, accumulating transferable knowledge that can adapt across different target models and evolving safety mechanisms. Rather than relying on static attack patterns, SRTJ dynamically generates candidate rules, selects promising combinations through constraint satisfaction, and refines strategies based on feedback from actual attack outcomes.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature on automated jailbreaking methods that have demonstrated vulnerabilities in safety-aligned LLMs despite alignment training. It responds to fundamental limitations in prior approaches: existing methods either lack systematic mechanisms for learning from failed attempts or fail to compose attack rules in principled ways that maintain reusability and transferability. The paper engages with research on adversarial attacks against neural systems, automated prompt optimization, and safety evaluation of language models. By introducing answer set programming as a mechanism for rule composition under constraints, it bridges insights from symbolic AI and constraint satisfaction with empirical adversarial optimization, drawing implicitly on work exploring the gap between safety training and actual robustness.
enables: This framework opens several future directions for both adversarial research and defense mechanisms. The approach of systematically accumulating and composing reusable attack rules could inform more robust safety testing procedures and help identify systematic vulnerabilities in alignment methods. Future work might extend this to develop better defensive strategies that account for rule-based compositional attacks. The training-free nature of the method suggests potential applications to black-box scenarios and rapidly evolving systems. Additionally, the ASP-based rule composition mechanism could inspire new defenses that explicitly account for adversarial rule composition, and research into safety mechanisms that can dynamically respond to and neutralize systematically discovered attack patterns.
